caption: Fig. 1. Density as a function of aluminum content for binary Fe-Al18 and for quaternary 0.2C-8.5Mn (wt.%) alloys studied in this work. The latter were evaluated by picnometry. The continuous line corresponds to the density calculation for the quaternary system using Thermo-Calc (see text for explanation).
paper_abstract: The current aim in the development of third-generation steels for lightweighting automotive applications is to increase strength keeping at least the same formability as current steel concepts. In this philosophy, an optimal concept would be one that brings, in addition, a lower density. For this purpose, low-density steels have been designed with important aluminum additions obtaining density reductions of 8–10% or higher in comparison with lowcarbon steels. At the levels required for lightweighting, aluminum introduces complex phenomena in steels. Here, some of the effects of aluminum in phase stability, CALPHAD-type modeling, and microstructure development are described, the latter in relation with mechanical properties. Finally, the potential of two families of lightweight steels for automotive applications is assessed by comparison with a steel currently present in automotive structures.
paper_title: Low-Density Steels: Complex Metallurgy for Automotive Applications
citation_sentences: Nevertheless, the effect of aluminum additions on density reduction of iron alloys was not mentioned, to the author’s knowledge, until Frommeyer et al.18 from Max-Planck-Institut fu¨ r Eisenforschung GmbH (MPIE) discussed in the late 1990s the reduction in density as a function of aluminum content for binary Fe-Al alloys (Fig. 1). This effect, discussed in the next section, has become, since then, an important design parameter in the development of steels for lightweighting automotive applications due to strict European Union regulations concerning $\mathrm { C O _ { 2 } }$ emissions for the coming years and alternative and competitive materials. Several new alternative concepts like carbide-free bainite, medium manganese steels, etc. could also bring an important reduction in the weight of vehicles because of the important levels of strength and elongation obtained. However, there is a hidden limit dictated by structural rigidity concepts. Therefore, an optimal concept would be one that brings in addition to an increased strength at iso-formability a lower density, allowing car makers to use higher thicknesses.

The experimental data (Fig. 1) seems to indicate a slightly lower density of quaternary alloys in comparison to the binary Fe-Al. There is probably an effect of Mn, but in this case the two data come from two different sources, which might also explain the difference. The Thermo-Calc calculation (Thermo-Calc Software, Inc., McMurray, PA, USA) was performed using the database with the latest assessment by Lindahl and Selleby19 with volume data from the commercial database Thermo-Calc

At low temperatures, thermodynamics predicts that the austenite in a two-phase structure should decompose to a certain degree into ferrite and the jphase (Fig. 2c). A good control of parameters and choice of chemistry could avoid this keeping a twophase austenite-ferrite structure at room temperature. To master the final microstructures, it is therefore required to understand austenite decomposition. $\mathrm { Z u a z o ^ { 3 5 } }$ has observed that the precipitation of this phase gives rise to particular microstructures at low Mn contents in which the similarity of crystal structures between austenite and the j-phase plays a significant role.

citation_sentences: In the first database, cementite is replaced at relatively low temperatures by $\bf { M } _ { 5 } C _ { 2 } ,$ which is not supported by the experiment. There are differences between the databases regarding the limits of composition of the different phase fields. For instance there is no agreement on the three-phase domain fcc + bcc + j, which is of primary importance during continuous annealing. Nevertheless, it is believed that the latest assessment should provide the best results. It should be pointed out that the Al-Mn-C system has not yet been evaluated and that the model for the j-phase needs to be improved. The information provided by these calculations is very important for the design of the composition and process. Figure 3 shows a comparison of austenite phase fractions using again this three databases. Similar results are observed between the first and the third version but are shifted in temperature. Experimental results are now needed in other phase fields than those used for the optimization to validate these new descriptions.

citation_sentences: The isothermal section of a FeMnAlC alloy with 8 wt.% Al at 850C in Fig. 4 shows the different phase fields occurring by varying C and Mn contents. The bcc phase dissolves a small but not negligible amount of C at lower Mn contents. This phase is still present at higher Mn contents as long as the C content is kept low. Increasing the C content promotes j-phase formation. In the cases where this phase is not desired, for instance during continuous annealing, a higher Mn content is required to avoid it. To reach a region where fcc is stable at this temperature, high C and Mn contents are required.

citation_sentences: Therefore, high-Al steels for automotive applications can be classified in three families: the ferritic alloys that are mainly Fe-Al alloys (Fig. 5a), the two-phase austenite-ferrite alloys in which C and Mn are incorporated in order to stabilize austenite (Fig. 5b, austenite in red), and the austenitic alloys with relatively high levels of C and Mn (Fig. 5c). This last one has been called TRIPLEX because of the additional presence of small amounts of ferrite (Fig. 5c—ferrite in blue) and the intragranular nanometric precipitation of the j-phase.

citation_sentences: Castan et al.34 studied the effects of strain, strain rate, and temperature on a ferritic Fe-8 wt.% Al alloy using hot torsion. The tests performed in the temperature range from $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $\bf { 1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } C }$ with a strain rate range of $0 . 1 \mathrm { - } 1 0 \ \mathrm { s } ^ { - 1 }$ showed that two types of dynamic recrystallization may operate during hot deformation: At a high temperature and high strain rate, this alloy undergoes discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX), whereas at a lower temperature and low strain rate, continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) occurs (Fig. 6).

citation_sentences: For instance, when cooling at low rates from the two-phase domain, the j-phase precipitates at the interface between austenite and ferrite. Figure 7a shows an example of the resulting microstructure with the j-phase and ferrite precipitating in a sort of nonlamellar eutectoid. The complexity of the morphology is better observed in three dimensions (Fig. 7b) by using a deep etching procedure. In this case, the j-phase growths along the long axis of austenite. Two other 3D morphologies of j-phase precipitation have been observed in this kind of steels using the same technique.35 At these low Mn contents, isothermal treatments in the two-phase ferrite j-phase domain promote a complete decomposition of austenite leading to a structure where a sort of cocoon of the j-phase is left (Fig. 7c).

citation_sentences: This precipitation has been studied in a 28.8Mn-9.4Al-0.92C (wt.%) alloy. Figure 8 shows the evolution of the microstructure observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) during aging at $\mathrm { 5 0 0 ^ { \circ } C }$ . The steel was initially solution treated at 1000C for 10 min and water quenched. Even at this initial state, diffuse superlattice reflections corresponding to the formation of intragranular j-phases were distinguished on the TEM diffraction pattern of austenite (Fig. 8a). After 2 min of aging, superlattice reflections caused by j-phases are still weak and diffuse. At this stage of aging, it is difficult to image j-phases by dark-field imaging. After 16 min of aging, j-phases are less diffuse and more intense, meaning that their volume fraction has increased (Fig. 8b). After 4 h of aging, they are clearly visible in dark-field imaging (Fig. 8c). They are round in shape and their size is smaller than 5 nm. At the longest time of aging (64 h) used in this study, jphases have a cuboidal shape with a size between 5 nm and 10 nm (Fig. 8d). They are arranged along the h100i directions of austenite.

caption: Fig. 9. Ferrite microstructure evolution of a 29Mn-9.6Al-0.56C (wt.%) during aging at 550C observed by TEM: Diffraction pattern of ferrite in the [011] zone axis and dark-field image using the {200} reflection are presented for (a) the as-quenched sample and (b) after 1 h of aging at 550C.

citation_sentences: During such aging, not only austenite can evolve. Indeed, ferrite shows some structure modifications. Ferrite evolution was observed in a 29Mn-9.6Al-0.56C (wt.%) alloy first treated at 850C for 10 min, water quenched, and then aged at 550C for 1 h. As can be seen on the TEM diffraction pattern of Fig. 9a, when ferrite is oriented in the [110] zone axis, there are extra spots corresponding to ordered structures. The {111} reflections indicate the presence of the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ structure, whereas the {200} reflections could correspond either to $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ or B2 structures. The difference of intensity between {111} reflections and {200} reflections indicates that the initial ferrite structure is a mix of B2- and

DO3-ordered structures. Such ordered structures are not only due to the high level of aluminum of the steel but also due to partitioning between austenite and ferrite that leads to a level of Al in ferrite higher than the nominal composition. After aging for 1 h at 550C (Fig. 9b), there is no more intensity difference between {111} reflections and {200} reflections on the diffraction pattern; ferrite becomes a fully ordered $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ structure.

citation_sentences: The change in properties seems to be continuous except for the samples treated at 800C. The observation of the microstructures shows that recrystallization of ferrite is not complete at 800C in comparison with that at 1000C (Fig. 11a and b). Ferrite grains in the shape of pancakes are mainly observed at the lower annealing temperatures. Nevertheless, it seems that local misorientations (not shown here) are similar, with inverse pole figure (IPF) maps showing a higher degree of variation in orientations at the lower temperatures. Recovery has probably already taken place to a certain degree.

citation_sentences: The effect of austenite grain size has been investigated for grain sizes ranging from 1.65 lm to 40 lm in a 28.8Mn-9.4Al-0.92C (wt.%) alloy. A classic Hall–Petch relation was found between (I) grain size and (II) yield strength and tensile strength with a coefficient of 461 MPa lm1/2 and 351 MPa lm1/2, respectively. In parallel, uniformelongation and total elongation increase when the grain size increases. However, a saturation effect is observed as soon as the grain size is higher than 10 lm. Figure 12 shows the evolution of the engineering stress–strain curves as a function of austenitic grain size.
